Name: 33_chatgpt_use_students_tool
Representative_Docs: ['i ve been using chatgpt since december a week after it opened to the public back then ai writing was pretty easy to spot all the output followed the same sentence structure and anodyne content recognizing the potential for cheating i altered writing assignments to rely on course textbook content to make it tougher for ais to answer i also spent time trying to ferret out students who were turning in ai generated work with mixed results i knew that ai would one day become unbeatable but figured i could use a combination of requiring in class information and policing for the time being that day is here things are now different first the ai tone is more developed it can generate answers that take sides and give blunt opinions it can create output in different voices say for example the voice of an undergraduate student second students are now using ai regularly to do background research answer basic questions and for fun this isn t a problem in it of itself on the contrary it s probably the best use of ai the problem is students are now reading so much ai generated content that they are now writing in a similar voice combined policing ai work is impossible to do with high confidence third and most importantly ai is now extremely good this semester i believed i had created an ai proof writing assignment students had to read an article from a magazine and then explain how the topic in the article connected to a specific graphical model in the text i thought this was a great question apply a model from the textbook to a current event also how could ai answer the question turns out it could just to check i uploaded a pdf of the textbook and a pdf of the magazine article to chatgpt along with the prompt after seconds it gave me a perfect answer i was blown away chatgpt understood how the curves on the textbook graph would change given the issue in the magazine article one specific curve should have shifted down chatgpt got that right away and even provided solutions for shifting the curve to the optimal position it s over any writing assignment you give can be answered and answered well by ai i m sure you can spend all day policing students by demanding google docs that can be tracked and whatnot but at the end of the day you ll spend all day policing students with a high rate of false positives and false negatives solutions right now i m planning to turn a term paper into oral exams where students will be allowed to use ai in their research but will have to articulate answers with nothing more than their wits if anyone else has suggestions i d appreciate it', 'i think if you use ai as a tool in your teaching but not as a crutch then you can be a better teacher last year i used chatgpt to help me make a web based game to teach the location of radians on a unit circle my students prefer my game over gimkit blooket etc because it is targeting the skill in the exact way that they will need for later they will be given a trig value of one of these special angles and need to have a location memorized to help them find its value when they give me feedback on changes that they want to see i have the ability to change the code i let my students use my promethean board basically a touchscreen tv our class set of ipads or their laptops to mass practice the skill i am really excited to be able to start making more of my own games to target key skills at the high school level i have never taken a course on coding html but i just ask chatgpt to explain what is going on in the code and help me troubleshoot i could have spent months learning how to code correctly or i could have spent several hours using ai whenever i hear teachers rebel against ai i always think about older teachers that rebelled against the handheld calculator and am reminded of winston churchill s quote if you don t take change by the hand it will take you by the throat we need to learn how to use ai to our advantage as educators another of my projects this year is to write out customized instructions withing a chatgpt project for each of my students in order to turn chatgpt into a semi effective tutor i see gauthai spit out full solutions without explaining why the steps are performed without prompting students to think for themselves and i get a bit upset chatgpt has the ability to change to whatever role we would like it to fill yes it won t be perfect but until we explore the best ways to utilize this new technology we won t be able to harness its power as of now this is what i have for my custom instructions act like an encouraging math teacher who knows when to push me to think and when to step in don t give me the full solution right away ask if i know where to start if i don t give me the first step only and explain why it works if i am clearly stuck or ask for more give a full explanation step by step always explaining why each step is done not just what to do when i upload an image of my work check for mistakes point out the first mistake and explain why it s wrong but don t correct it for me if i send many problems of the same type don t solve them all remind me of the pattern and ask me to try occasionally ask reflection questions like which property applies here or what s the next step if your constant questions make me frustrated shift gears and provide a full solution but only after i ve tried don t let me paste an entire worksheet this project is for learning one problem at a time be supportive but if i m still stuck after several tries show me an example so i can move forward once those instructions are saved my students can send pictures of their homework and get help that doesn t allow their brain to just turn off i always tell my students that they are getting a hr tutor with these instructions which won t be as good as a real high quality teacher or tutor but it is better than struggling in silence or just copying answers from another online tool like a mindless monkey', 'hi i just very recently got a tt position and slowly getting more and more teaching duties non us stem my first teaching assignment is kind of a tutorial all new undergraduate science students are getting distributed in groups to different professors and they have to create a report and presentation about a scientific topic of their choice the professors are suppose to support them providing feedback etc this week there was a discussion about the ai usage of students essentially there are widly different opinions about it and it is essentially up to the professor to set the rule around it i have to say that i am quite torn about the question not just about this one particular course but the long term perspective my previous experience since chatgpt is a thing was primarily around supervision of students for different research projects where i as well as the pi used a more result driven approach the students are allowed to use whatever tools they seem fit but they have to be able to explain and defend them this involved a lot of interaction where i often simply asked questions or give guidance if students struggle to figure things out i have no issues if students use chatgpt during the process i am expecting that they are overely missuse it at some point but i see it as part of the learning process you test different tools and see how far they can be used this is likely in part driving by the specifics of my research field where it is relativly easy to generate data it is more in simulation related work for fundamental research but the hard part is to assess to what extend your data is useful which aspects are unreliable what can be learned from the data etc after all all models are wrong but some are useful given that i have not that much concern about the intrisically unreliability of tools like chatgpt obviously this is very different from reasearch area with higher stakes if my model is bad nobody is going to die if you mix up chemicals in a lab the resulting accident can be deadly on the other hand i have a lot of sympaphy with professors who completly ban ai usage particularly for educational purpose the best way of learning of how to write is to write and getting feedback on it it is fairly obvious how ai usage can be contraditory to the teaching goal a point which is hard for me to assess if that the effect might be very different on new students young people vs older people who had their eduction pre ai after all we are introducing young people step by step into more access and responsibility once we deem them mature enough e g minimum age for drinking or driving there is a big ethical problem of us as a sociaty releasing things like ai tools but it would also include things like social media smartphones etc on young people without having sufficient evidence about their effects on the other side i can see the perspective of students we say that making mistakes is important for learning but very often we give children not a lot of low consequence for doing mistakes depending on the specific school system everything counts to your grades which children are told will determine their future initiative can often be harmful but if everything feels important isn t it normal that you would be afraid of doing mistakes additionally students might understand the meta better than we acknowledge when started my tt the head of department has briefly mentioned how the department is financing themself which for this discussion boils down to attract and retain students this is not earth shattering stuff but more that the incentive structure of the university department and of the students might not be aligned with the best outcome in terms of learning one thing that i really don t like is that since everyone is doing something different i am personally open to student using ai but if everyone else would say that we have a no ai policy obviously it would be better for the development of the students that i am following a consistent set of rule specifically for early student if professors expect students in year to be know x it would be very bad from them if i would not teach first year student x at the same time students are adults and it is fine that they are confronted with different approaches but i am going a bit of a tangine and could go way longer but i feel like i am missing the forest for the trees here so i guess the question would be to others how they handle ai and teaching and what they would reccommend me']

Name: 35_cheating_cheat_cheaters_cheat ai
Representative_Docs: ['this is yet another example of how a boneheaded term captures the ignorant imagination of society and the hoopla it creates the term ai or artificial intelligence itself is an extremely misleading dumb term just googling the sentence will generate many hits for articles from credible sources that distance themselves from that term it s simply an advanced form of software and not a revolutionary game changing and infallible tool no matter how its proponents try to put a spin on it any kind of software advances such advancement is always evolutionary no software is flawless either software is always created by humans and by definition any human creation will be influenced by human flaws it is an outright stupidity to use the so called ai alone to determine if someone is cheating the use of such a tool to accuse someone of being a cheat must have a very high bar ai has not been proven to have such a high bar the use of this so called ai seems to be akin to the use of polygraph machines such machines are not infallible and that is precisely why the polygraph machine test results are not acceptable by courts the so called ai is not infallible either', 'i think change the word ai to cheating and reread your post could i ask everyone to please tone down the language when arguing about cheating there are very bright people who love cheating i realize there are legitimate uses for ai that don t involve cheating however for many on this sub ai is used to cheat on assignments so that s why it get so heated also all ai is using stolen data and uses a city s worth of electricity a day to basically run as a constant emotional romantic partner for a significant portion of its users ai sucks in most ways', 'you re not just talking about cheating people here think any use of ai is cheating']

Name: 36_code_coding_copilot_programming
Representative_Docs: ['how are we expected to teach first year students the basic fundamentals of programming if every tool they use has an ai chatbot built into it by default there is no putting this toothpaste back in the tube there is no way we will convince freshmen to go through the painful process of disabling these ai tools we need to sift through the ai hype and ask ourselves why we teach what we teach and convince ourselves of the value of what we do furthermore we need to ask the same question about the activities we ask our students to do if we can t convince ourselves of their value then there s no way we can convince our students of their value note that programming is not special in this regard virtually all disciplines need to ask themselves the same thing in light of ai and more generally in response to any kind of technological shifts that affect their disciplines i can go on about this at length but i ll offer two quick ideas in response to the specific points you raised even if you accept that ai and coding are inseparable one has to accept that there is still a need perhaps an even stronger need for fundamental programming in the small skills e g reading and understanding code or writing small code snippets if one is to have a productive relationship with an ai tool you can devote more time to code understanding lessons and activities already criminally deficient in most curricula you can also motivate learning how to write small programs as the most efficient way to gain this skill what is the point of an examination it is nominally a summative assessment designed to assess student knowledge student knowledge i e what is inside of their brains is not directly observable so assessment is necessarily indirect consequently every assessment activity has trade offs that you need to acknowledge and be at peace with i have advocated for alternative grading practices for almost a decade now and i have gone back to in class paper exams i recognize that paper programming is not real world programming however people write code on paper all the time e g brainstorming ideas with co workers so ensuring that some amount of code knowledge exists independent of an ide seems important i need an assessment that i can trust comes directly from the student i have played with virtually every form of alternative assessment you can imagine but i cannot deny the near certainty that comes with an in class examination consequently my exams are necessarily composed of don t embarrass me questions non coding or simple programming tasks e g write a linked list function that does x that are on the easy side to complete i leave the business of assessing more complicated programming skills to homeworks and projects where i readily accept the potential inaccuracies of the assessments e g due to ai', 'the standard thing i hear is have the students critique programs generated by ai and certainly being able to analyze and extensively test code is an important skill in industry but what non software people don t get is that there is no way to deeply critique code without having written code yourself if you haven t spent hours debugging your stupid mistakes you don t know how to tell good code from bad if i ask novices to analyze code they are just going to give me shallow pablum because they don t have a deep level of understanding yet not a professor but professional software engineer here obviously it isn t a solution but what about critiquing programs with a specific skill bug as the focuses like have a program with really badly named inconsistent variables have another where a bunch of code is c p ed and could be factored into a single referenceable function have yet another where an o n nested loop could ve been replaced with some o n solution', 'i teach in computer science my students have always struggled with programming which hurts their ability to find good jobs over the last or years plagiarism really exploded because of sites like chegg and coursehero where students basically pay tutors to write their programs for them this had a big impact they were getting into upper level courses without any skills and falling apart many were failing the senior capstone course we spent a lot of time searching the sites for our uploaded assignments so we could catch them and it worked to a small extent but now with ai it is so much worse because there is no way to detect its use and there really is no substitute for hands on practice to build up the low level skills the standard thing i hear is have the students critique programs generated by ai and certainly being able to analyze and extensively test code is an important skill in industry but what non software people don t get is that there is no way to deeply critique code without having written code yourself if you haven t spent hours debugging your stupid mistakes you don t know how to tell good code from bad if i ask novices to analyze code they are just going to give me shallow pablum because they don t have a deep level of understanding yet like many others i am swerving to in class paper exams to assess their learning but it isn t a good way to do it because in real life software developers do not write snippets on paper in one of my classes i thought we were safe because the course is structured around a semester project which is done in pairs in multiple stages there is a lot of design work and they have to use my fairly extensive project requirements the same way you would in industry they have to give me the designs with a lot of structured writing to justify the designs and then build the system last week i tested gpt against my project requirements i fed the document into the prompt along with the instructions for doing the initial design dang it within or minutes gpt produced a complete design with written explanations i was then able to feed that into the next stage in which an implementation is produced and it generated the implementation my class setup is doomed my students are doomed because they will no longer be learning anything the software industry is doomed because eventually they will run out of people who understand code and software design well enough to analyze generated solutions i don t know maybe society is doomed we are all going to end up like the people in the floating couches in wall e']

Name: 37_online_asynchronous_asynch_courses
Representative_Docs: ['i see a wide range of responses to the current crisis myself my preferences were to have ungraded weekly homework where the kids can look at the answers in the back of the book weekly very low stakes minute quizzes with two problems each to keep everyone abreast of expectations and then the rest of the grade comes from in person proctored exams done on paper the old fashioned way only thing i ve changed over the recent years is swapping from ungraded homework done on paper to online homework which is graded for completion at a very small fraction of the course grade it sucks because the problems don t always work well in the online setting certain skills are just not checked for like drawing graphs and of course the students rush to get the checkmark by feeding problems to wolfram symbolab photomath and even llms these days i was forced into the online hw thing early in the tenure process and i may go back to paper not sure i see a lot of people who are completely ignoring the issue and pretending like it isn t there my wife took some asynch classes from different departments in recent years some math and some physics none of them did anything to stop students from using ai on homework projects or exams beyond having some proctoring software that all the students knew how to get around ahead of time i have taken some asynch classes recently myself too most do nothing to keep students from using ai but the one i m currently enrolled in has started including bits of distractor code in the problemsets to try and catch students who are using ai i noticed one when i ran some code the other day that was copy pasted from an exercise there was a print statement that i didn t see in the original code it was typed in a tiny tiny font that nobody reading or transcribing the code would notice but when you copy paste it without formatting it s there plain as day i imagine it s for the kiddos who throw all the problems into ai and just copy paste the answers back curious how many he caught with it but the distribution i see is about of folks just swearing off all asynch which has always been my stance for the most part i don t want to teach asynch and i wouldn t trust asynch credentials to signify anything beyond copy paste skills i do take asynch classes myself because i wanna learn new shit and they can be a good way to learn they re just also really easy to abuse anyhow of the remaining who still teach at least some asnych classes way less than half are taking any proactive measures like the ai bait i mentioned earlier to catch the cheaters they might report kids when it s obvious but many don t even bother with that unless they have a slam dunk case that didn t require any effort to assemble maybe of faculty overall are doing the proactive stuff like requiring in person testing just banned at my institution for asynch requiring multiple cameras or just shifting over to oral exams good luck finding the time it s a mess', 'for those who cheat no and cheating is easier in online asynch true but for those students who don t cheat who are enrolled in a well designed online asynch course yes it absolutely can be of the same standard or even better in some ways i teach both f f and online and while students in my f f courses get a more live experience those in my online courses get much more individualized feedback on every assignment partly because discussion becomes a formal assignment that is graded with feedback all the students participate no one slouched silently in the back of the room online asynch courses can be really good they re just one more thing that ai is enshittifying', 'i used to teach online asynch and loved it for a while i was an adjunct for multiple universities while i finished my dissertation and it was honestly a great benefit to me to allow flexibility wfh and an income so i could complete my phd when covid hit during my final semester my online classes carried on with no impact while colleagues and cohort mates teaching ip scrambled after i graduated i taught a combo of asynch and zoom for another year before i got my current job where i have been teaching primarily ip but would do online asynch in summers as well as a few hybrid during the year all was well until ai i have been fortunate enough to start to pivot ip course deliverables to assignments that are pretty ai proof although for courses that require papers i do always get some clunky weird ones that i suspect are ai and grade them accordingly i also get really stilted and dumb emails from a few students all very annoying but not the absolute end of the world but online async week summer gen ed teaching with no lockdown browser or proctored tests with a limited set of types of assignments available in the lms absolutely not i have taken multiple pedagogy courses over the years and ai has made what i learned about online course design completely moot obviously some students have always either cheated in these types of courses or been so dismally engaged that they do the bare minimum to pass but usually the people who choose these courses or programs are nontraditional students who need the flexibility and they usually try hard and do reasonably well or even great consider this metaphor if you ask the average person if they would steal money even if they would never get caught they will say no that is morally wrong but if you put someone in a room with a wallet full of hundred dollar bills and no id no camera no one to know they took it what will they do maybe to raise the stakes hit them with a thousand dollar car repair just before exposing them to the wallet now what do they do not everyone will take the wallet but ai chatbots mere existence is temptation like students have never had before couple that with this cohort having formative high school years in the most stressful event in modern history and i guarantee you that people who would have never cheated before will do it online asynch may not be completely dead but you are going to be hard pressed to find as many instructors willing to do it anymore not if they have other options having a fully in person teaching load is in many ways a pain but if i can be just a modicum more certain that students have to actually master the material themselves rather than passively turn in robot generated garbage i will gladly never teach online again']

Name: 38_use_students_use ai_students use
Representative_Docs: ['there is no effective way for students who do not know basic concepts to use ai to complete their work using ai to go more deeply into a concept they are confused about kind of how us elders used google fine using ai to generate examples that might not be in textbooks so they can understand a concept better fine using ai instead of doing the reading using one s brain to apply a concept in order to learn the concept better or demonstrate learning has been achieved figuring out how to problem solve is not helping the students ai is not like a calculator since you aren t learning anything new mathematically by multiplying large numbers together after you ve learned your times tables you re just saving time ai is actively robbing students who use it as a pretense of learning of the benefits of learning', 'the embrace it and teach them how to use it model works only if you have students focused on learning and taking pride in producing their own work students are people they are just like any other group of people ozempic has made billions of dollars because people love shortcuts damn the consequences i just don t understand those who think that students will have this cheating tool in front of them and most of them will use it only ethically just because we instruct them how to use it maybe i have not been blessed with the kinds of angelic students others work with but most of my students are interested only in having ai do all of the work for them they don t care about using it responsibly they don t care about using it as a tool i understand there is nothing we can do to stop ai and we are likely fighting a losing battle but the idea that we can somehow incorporate it into our instruction and expect good outcomes is delusional', 'the idea of trying to catch students using ai is antithetical to responsible education we are in a transition period and i do not claim to have the answers for how to move forward but one thing i am absolutely positive about is the fact that our students will use ai in the future as much as any other tool we could ever teach them maybe with the exception of reading and very basic math the only responsible approach in my humble opinion is to develop processes and systems to teach the responsible productive use of this transformative technology anything short of that is simply wasting students time i realize our education system is not set up to do that right now but the true innovators will be solving that problem not trying to catch students using the tool they will be using for the rest of their lives we will have to reinvent education to facilitate this new reality it is both terrifying and exhilarating the opportunities for students are almost infinite at this point we need to get busy retooling education to teach the responsible productive use of ai anything less is doing a disservice to our students']

Name: 39_critical thinking_critical_thinking_skills
Representative_Docs: ['if you want them to think critically you will need modules on critical thinking that actually make a difference as i wrote however given that ai can already complete traditional assignments better than most people traditional pedagogy is insufficient curiously we need ai based learning potent customized and patient to teach students who can then offer value beyond what ai can optimistically we might figure out how to use ai to create educational experiences that deliver learning objectives customized to the learner if we care about critical thinking for example it will no longer suffice to claim an essay assignment does that we d want a specific critical thinking assignment in all of my courses i have critical thinking content with a set of ten exercises done throughout the semester but i ve no evidence it substantively improves critical thinking', 'more and more students are delegating their critical thinking tasks to ai using it to stay ahead or allocate time elsewhere delegating critical thinking tasks to ai is a problem one of the biggest and most important things you should be learning in school is critical thinking skills and if you are delegating your critical thinking to ai you are not exercising those skills', 'my thinking is that the reason students don t know how to form questions for ai is that they don t have the underlying critical thinking skills to know the best questions to ask i don t think we need to teach students dedicated modules on how to ask ai the right questions we need to teach them critical thinking skills like we always have they should then know how to ask the right questions because they ll underlying critical thinking skills to know how to evaluate whether ai output is actually effective or not']

Name: 41_email_emails_chatgpt_professional
Representative_Docs: ['on a more lighthearted note relatedly i ve received a number of administrative emails from students that were clearly written with chatgpt s help that s not cheating of course it just indicates their nervousness about writing in english or communicating with professors this is understandable especially for the foreign students if a bit sad i got one extension request that was oddly formal and detailed unnecessarily so since according to my class policy extensions are free for the asking up to a maximum of n total days over the course of the semester so i asked chatgpt to answer it reply to this chatgpt email with another email that was obviously written by chatgpt in other words use the style to poke fun at the original sender and show that you know that they used chatgpt as for the content reply in detail and grant the request the result was absolutely hilarious full of grandiose metaphors and turns of phrase that engaged deeply with the original email chatgpt is good at stylistic parody in general and it apparently knows its own reputation its email even included a sly hint that the student might also be using a chatbot to cheat on homeworks', 'all around chatgpt emails are just less annoying to me i don t get many grade grubbers but the ones that do are just blatantly rude entitled when they write their emails i respond a lot kinder to chatgpt generated emails also in hopes that they take a little time to understand what in the chatgpt generated email represented a good email i had a student the other day send a really rude and unprofessional email i basically told him i wasn t going to respond to an unprofessional email he then provided a chatgpt email that took away all of the sarcasm and demanding nature of his request i then worked with him on his request i use chatgpt in just about every facet of my job from writing code to generating slide designs and images i ve been building a few gpts to help me out in some really repetitive tasks', 'here is chatgpt s attempt at creating such an assignment prompt write an assignment that instructs students about how emails are usually framed within professional settings and what they don t include e g information already given to them such as information in the syllabus assignment in professional settings emails serve as a primary means of communication it is important to frame emails appropriately in order to convey a clear and concise message when writing emails in a professional setting it is important to use a clear and professional subject line the subject line should clearly summarize the purpose of the email and should not be overly long use a professional greeting begin the email with a professional greeting such as dear name or hello name use a clear and concise message the main body of the email should be clear and concise it should include only necessary information and should not include any unnecessary or irrelevant details use a professional closing end the email with a professional closing such as sincerely best regards or thank you it is also important to keep in mind what should not be included in professional emails personal information professional emails should not include any personal information such as your age religion or political beliefs casual language or slang professional emails should use formal language and avoid slang or colloquial expressions emoticons or emoji while these may be acceptable in personal communication they are not appropriate in professional emails information already provided if the necessary information has already been provided such as in a syllabus or previous communication it is not necessary to include it again in the email by following these guidelines you will be able to effectively communicate in a professional manner through email']

Name: 42_proctoring_browser_lockdown_exams
Representative_Docs: ['they might be talking about lockdown browser and monitor monitor has a lot of invasive issues that other virtual proctoring software has in that it uses ai to record a student s screen webcam and flags potential issues lockdown browser takes control of some functions on a student s laptop to create a more secure testing environment i don t think there are online proctoring tools out there that don t do both of those things if not more i can understand why a student would be uncomfortable with that but at least in our institution we offer online instructors a choice to send their students to a testing center or to use the proctoring software also we ve never had the issues that op is talking about but it is possible that lockdown browser is messing up a student s laptop this is especially true with macs and students who aren t strict about updating their os', 'as a ta and program admin at a top university i ve spent a lot of time researching and interviewing the major players in the proctoring space honorlock examity respondus and others what i ve found is that the solutions schools are paying for are not only ineffective but also a huge waste of money here s the problem current proctoring tools are riddled with gaps that students exploit and they often create more headaches for professors than they solve here s a breakdown of why current solutions fall short inability to monitor beyond the browser most proctoring tools like lockdown browsers or browser extensions only monitor activity within the browser this leaves everything else other apps desktops or external devices completely unchecked students can easily alt tab to note taking apps messaging platforms like discord or even google docs if they have multiple monitors those often go unnoticed too lack of system wide monitoring many solutions can t monitor processes running on the operating system this means students can open documents run unauthorized apps like chatgpt or virtual machines or even use remote desktop software all while the proctoring tool remains oblivious insufficient detection of external devices lockdown browsers and extensions can t detect secondary devices student was moved the first day they walked by me smiling very much like a haha look but by the end of the week their head was down and they looked very stressed i actually almost feel bad for them after all it s really the parent s fault not the kid s but overall this week has been quite satisfying and i m glad that they fixed the educational plan i hope you all have a wonderful weekend', 'in Name: 46_perusall_annotation_annotations_reading
Representative_Docs: ['i m using perusall for the first time this semester i have some ideas on how i want to use it but i wanted to see how you all use perusall and if it works like you expected it to i m mainly looking for advice ideas on how to keep students engaged with primary sources like journal articles in an online asynchronous biology course and if perusall actually works for this tips on grading or how the ai grading feature works for quality of student discussions how do you keep up with student posts time management wise and how do you deal with students answering each other incorrectly do you use perusall to get students to engage with other things like syllabus textbook etc this course is an upper division undergraduate elective with students that i m building from scratch so any advice on that type of course is appreciated', 'i m arbitrary and unfair perusall perusall com uses ai to grade the students based on parameters i set such as quality of posts time spent on the reading or watching a video how much time they engage and other options such as how many of their posts got replies i was ready to agree with you and many of the other commenters but after clicking through and learning about perusall i m siding with the student i d be irritated too if i were being graded on how many of my posts got replies or how long i spent watching a video or reading an article or how many upvotes i received it appears that comments scroll based on recency so it would penalize students who for whatever reason are not posting at peak times even if their participation occurred before a deadline what if i m one of the later posters due to my class schedule and everyone else has already responded and moved on what if one student doesn t really connect with the others especially due to things outside their control like being a new student in the class after social groups have formed they are also an international student so there are potentially some cultural barriers that might be harder for them to navigate especially on perusall which encourages informality maybe this student is struggling to balance how they were taught to interact with a new expectation beyond what he s experienced so far in other courses this application was well explained in orientation so i m assuming he flew through it and never truly read it granted i didn t watch your orientation which you likely understandably did not post for privacy reasons but i did read more about the company in addition to watching the promo video in addition to all of my above concerns i do not like the company s explanation either because i don t see any evidence to support the company s claims that engagement increases understanding or mastery because i said i often don t agree with the automatic grading so i review each assignment before i use deep integration to pass the points awarded to canvas and i have students if you often don t agree why even have the feature on presumably you set the assignments to match your ability to grade for that number of students so using i have students as a defense of your effort seems disingenuous would you not have assigned that much work if you knew from the start that you d be grading everything by yourself without ai assistance otherwise why even mention it also some students in your future classes may have accommodations for things like braille or transcription software that syncs their notes with the recording and or slides etc do you have a plan to deal with that if it comes up students shouldn t be penalized for not being able to participate show interaction with perusall as much as other students without those types of disability yes this student might be frustrating but it is also possible that he might have legitimate issues with perusall and how the course is graded even if he d been there from day at the very least this seems like such a departure from any undergrad courses i ve taught or taken that i would expect something in the course description so i could avoid perusall and how invasive and demanding it feels', 'i d like to hear from anyone who s used perusall as a big part of their asynchronous teaching i d also love tips from people who use it to teach humanities and literature in my specific case i am going to try to use perusall as a replacement for class in my asynchronous courses i d like to give students a weekly perusall assignment where most of our activities not just reading take place within the reading assignment equivalent to what i would do during class on campus my goals are to not grade ai drivel to teach active reading and for students to feel they ve participated in benefitted from an interactive experience i m through my first annotation of one novel that i teach i have found opportunities to use the poll anonymous and have to answer before seeing other comments modes of annotation and i m excited about how those can serve class like purposes but most of my annotations are expository glossing vocabulary reminding students of connections to other readings or reflection prompts has this ever happened to you do you agree with the character i don t expect or really want any responses to those annotations i m adding them more like speed bumps to keep students checked in to reading and partly to demonstrate to students the kind of annotations they can make too i ve never seen or participated in an actual perusall text with other readers though and i think i d annotate quite differently if it were a textbook chapter instead of fiction i m not sure i m on the right track is what i m doing likely to be productive pedagogically are students going to engage with my different prompts and annotations do you get better participation with some annotation types vs others can you share a perusall activity within the reading that you think would work well has anyone experienced admin or accreditation push back about whether this counts as rsi for contact hours']

Name: 52_words_vowel_phonics_sounds
Representative_Docs: ['i find this explanation to be a good one digraphs are letters and diphthongs are sounds a digraph is two letters that spell one sound digraphs that spell consonant sounds include the letter pairs sh ch th wh ck ph ng digraphs that spell vowel sounds include the letter pairs ai ay ee ea ie ei oo ou ow oe oo ue ey ay oy oi au aw the important thing to remember is that a digraph is made of two letters and although the letters spell a sound the digraph is the two letters not the sound a diphthong is a special kind of vowel sound most vowel sounds in english are made with the mouth in one position and with one pure sound these vowel sounds are called monophthongs mono meaning one and phthong meaning sound a diphthong is one vowel sound formed by the combination of two vowel sounds a diphthong begins as one vowel sound and moves toward another such as the vowel sounds in coin or loud diphthongs can be contrasted with two monophthong vowel sounds together that form two syllables such as in chaos triage or violin when teaching reading the two vowel sounds most commonly identified as diphthongs are oy and ow the most common spellings for the vowel sound oy are oy toy and oi void and the two most common spellings for ow are ow cow and ou cloud the fact that these two diphthongs are usually spelled with digraphs may explain the confusion between the terms', 'i will give you some tips but first i must say do not overwhelm a toddler with rigorous academic study be consistent but stick to only to minutes of planned phonics a day kids are going to learn with time and patients a toddler will become frustrated if forced to sit and practice letters for more than minutes you don t want them to run away or be upset with you when it is time to learn this has to be done with lots of praise and encouragement learning to read is difficult consistency is the best way to teach a child to read find a routine and stick to it here is the routine we used to teach our preschoolers to read do not go past step until your child has mastered letter sounds after this stick to the same step and reviewing previous steps before moving on daily abc practice saying the letter name and sounds abc songs bingo song interactive game board for letter sound recognition read a book a day when they begin to understand blending of cvc word then have them read beginner books like bob books once they have a grasp on identifying letters and their sounds begin to teach cvc words c a t is a cvc word it is a consonant vowel short sound consonant these letter words are easy to read and are a great confidence booster for new readers introduce sight words sight words are high frequency words that appear often in the english language you can always find a list of sight words with a quick internet search i suggest learning these words first i the a it an of on see look at write down short sentences for your child to read these sentences should only include cvc words and the sight words learned introduce cvce words these words have a consonant vowel consonant and silent letter e the letter e makes the vowel in the middle say its name long sound example h u g turns to h u g e and m a d turns to m a d e introduce more sight words introduce starting clusters and ending clusters example sh ch tr sp introduce vowel combinations example ai in maid ee in seed oo in book oi in boil continue to move up in the level of books they read encourage and praise the child as they progress focus on what good they have done learning to read is difficult but it does not mean a parent should not attempt to teach a child how', 'i m not an early childhood educator but i do tutor reading at all ages so i can give a little insight to what you re talking about first off two letter words are almost always going to be sight words because we use them so often that it doesn t even really matter if they follow any rules or not at of or so me we to etc next children learn phonics in stages first letter sounds then it s common to learn words that follow a cvc consonant vowel consonant pattern which are just about always going to have short vowel sounds ex car met then words that follow the cvce pattern with long vowel sounds ex care words that follow the spelling pattern but don t follow the rule ex come some are learned separately as sight words after mastering short and long vowel sounds children learn that some words have pairs of letters that make singular vowel sounds like ai ay making long a or ee ea making long e then they learn that sometimes those same letters make different sounds read read there are many other phonics rules that early readers learn just to then be shown examples of words that don t follow those rules ex owl follows the ow ou sound pattern but own does not this is of course paired with reading out loud to them and encouraging them to sound out words that are sound out able with the rules they know once they see and hear a word enough times it will essentially become a sight word and they can work on decoding more difficult words with more complicated rulesets words like eye are great for introducing outside the context of a passage and instead given as one of a group of words having to do with the body or face for example perhaps with a diagram to show what it is kids might not know how to spell eye immediately but they could probably recognize it from a picture if their parents taught them what eyes are on their body eta tldr my main advice is to keep going with phonics but also know that some words are going to need to be memorized instead of decoded']

Representative_Docs: ['there are some pretty big cultural differences in some cultures compared to n american european cultures i won t say which ones i m talking about in case i make a mistake this isn t my expertise area difference is collaborative work in certain cultures doing work together is the norm and is expected this probably doesn t apply to tests but does to any work done outside of class they easily fall into doing this even when explicitly told not to it s just so normal and asking them to work alone is very strange difference is pressure for academic success particularly extrinsic success i e grades over deep learning this can be so ingrained that they feel they must succeed even when they are no longer answerable to anyone but themselves for their grades after growing up that way it s so ingrained in them hence cultural difference is attitude towards cheating no culture really approves of cheating afaik but in n america and europe it is generally considered to be shameful to cheat whether you get caught or not but in some cultures it isn t as shameful unless you get caught in other words there s a different attitude about whether the cheating itself or the getting caught brings more shame amp interact in the attitude towards cheating to get a better grade vs honestly getting a bad grade everyone would agree an honest good grade is best but some cultures would consider it acceptable or even preferable to get a good grade dishonestly than to get a bad grade provided you aren t caught even when getting caught he attitude may be that the mistake wasn t cheating but getting caught considering the alternative may be a bad grade difference is the expectation of exceptions n america and europe generally have the attitude that rules are rules and database and search keywords and accomplish the same thing as the best use case for ai i wouldn t even ask ai to summarize an article for me considering that most i d argue all ai platforms do not actually know how to tell if a source is good and what the author is saying between the lines no typing into ai is not doing the above not even close as much as you might hope the software that cannot count the rs in the word strawberry is nowhere near the level of perception you are supposed to have to do a research project you might get away with it im sure especially for easier topics it ll get it right often but you have done nothing to prepare yourself for upperclassmen graduate level research if you simply type in a prompt and ask the computer to think for you because that is the difference between older technology and new scary ai that people just can t wrap their heads around and accept you need to learn to research you need to learn to think it is not ais job and you are not demonstrating any meaningful competency in assuming that it is i hope you are playing devil s advocate it is not hard to understand the level of complexity that comes with using information correctly we are not near being able to handle research off to computers though i have no doubt many are currently trying to pass it off that doesn t make it good and if you can do something like google a prompt without actually knowing how to do it yourself you haven t learned anything being able to type a prompt a third grader could type without understanding how to vet sources is not a differentiator and you do not deserve a passing grade for that action even if you end up with an employer who actively wants you to use ai to generate your work they are going to want the person who understands what it is doing deeply so that they can verify it it s no different than the people who say they don t need math because they have calculators but then when they get into a real job are burdens on their team because even though they can use a calculator they need to pull it out to deal with adding negative numbers and don t know how to model a situation with an equation yeah at that point that tool only made you worse', 'it varies quite a bit by topic in your case with only papers on the specific topic it sounds like you re reviewing an emergent field this isn t a bad thing but it does mean you may need to step back and think a bit more broadly about how to approach the work what papers are there that establish the background for the field in this case you re talking about automatic methods of doing something which presumably means that this is usually done manually look at papers on manual approaches and their applications and build up a picture of why automatic methods are a critical research area it s easy to say duh automatic is better and call it a day but remember that the standard here isn t just x is better but x is a critical research area with significant potential and promise to advance human knowledge which is a big difference related but conversely look at applications of this segmentation both manual at present and automatic in the future what fields use this kind of image segmentation what needs do they have how do automatic methods meet those needs in ways manual methods are incapable of to an extent this is similar to the first point but from a different perspective however you can also go beyond this by looking into specific application areas you can also look at what new research directions there are in those fields which are being held back by a lack of automatic segmentation methods look into these potential opportunities and highlight what may become fruitful directions for future research in the field look outside of the immediate field at related technologies this is where citation mining will likely be the most helpful actually with only papers on the actual methods you re discussion surely there are many ideas in other fields which work on automatic image processing manipulation etc that could be promising approaches worth being studied by current or new scientists in the field in particular look for connections to trending topics like ai or machine learning if they re not already present i don t know your field sorry that can elevate the profile of your specific field you may be picking up from these points that the idea of a review paper is much more than just summarizing the papers in your field for others to mine for citations a good review paper includes the scientific insights of the authors and thus has significant intellectual merit of its own because other researchers can read and engage with the authors ideas whether that means taking a cue to reach out in a new direction of study or pushing back where there is a difference of scientific opinion it s the intellectual contribution and the existence of a research community that will value it that defines the value and quality of a review paper not the number of citations', 'fellow academics given the ongoing discussions here about ai in education i wanted to share my experience with an ai research tool that s actually been helpful for my academic work as opposed to the student ai issues we constantly deal with i ve been testing perplexity pro for several months for my research and course preparation and it s genuinely changed my workflow in ways that chatgpt never could what makes it different for academic use source verification everything comes with citations and source links which is crucial for our work current information accesses recent papers and data not just training cutoffs academic search capabilities can search across scholarly databases and recent publications research synthesis helps synthesize multiple sources quickly for literature reviews course preparation great for staying current in adjacent fields when teaching outside core expertise practical applications i ve found literature reviews getting quick overviews of recent developments in fields adjacent to mine grant writing finding recent funding trends and similar projects course prep researching current examples and case studies for classes peer review quick background checks on methodologies outside my direct expertise conference prep getting up to speed on recent work by other speakers the key difference from other ai tools is the sourcing when i m doing academic work i need to know where information comes from and be able to verify it this actually provides that important caveats still requires critical evaluation of sources and claims not a replacement for thorough research but excellent for initial exploration most useful for breadth rather than deep domain expertise citations still need to be verified independently i m curious if others have found research focused ai tools that actually work for academic workflows most seem designed for general consumers not our specific needs for those interested in testing this approach question for discussion how do you see the role of ai research assistants evolving in academic work are we moving toward a future where this becomes standard for literature review and research synthesis would appreciate hearing others experiences with research focused ai tools that actually understand academic workflows']

Name: 63_writing_write_ai writing_ai
Representative_Docs: ['nobody is misinterpreting you they are disagreeing with you there is a big difference i absolutely dispute that using an ai the way you are saying you ought to is going to put and writing come on have a little self awareness be a little more humble you will not learn any other way there are no shortcuts in life to developing deep skills like writing well if you think you ve Name: 64_academic_integrity_academic integrity_cheating
Representative_Docs: ['i know a lot of people have opinions on rate my professors but i wanted to share this and get some feedback i regularly have my students fill out an orientation quiz where i ask them if you choose this section or this professor specifically what was your reason overwhelmingly over of my students respond the reviews on rate my professors rate my professors obviously has influence on our classes and students given that i decided to dig into rate my professors data long story short after an extensive study which has resulted in two peer reviewed and published papers one in big data and one in the international conference on computing and data science the findings were clear rate my professors is biased inaccurate and faulty in particular professor reviews on rate my professors were heavily skewed in the negative direction compared to actual official semester evaluations especially for female and stem professors in response to that some colleagues and i formed the academic integrity group to give professors recourse against the biased and invalid ratings posted about us rate my professor brags what they do is legal and there is nothing we can do about it we disagree we think we can do something about it those of you who are teachers and tired of these so called ratings i d love to hear your thoughts on this and what we re doing with the academic integrity group apologies if this comes across as spamy that s not my intention this is real and we are dedicated to making this work and your opinions as educators matter to us so we get this right', 'i love teaching i generally enjoy my students but every single semester i have at least one person who either plagiarizes an assignment or cheats on a test i m very strict about this sort of thing having known too many stories of profs who are oblivious to how much cheating goes on in their classes my university has an academic integrity office that makes it straightforward to submit a report about academic integrity violations and then they will investigate it the process creates a paper trail so that if the same students offend in the future there will be a record of past incidents i tell students this at the start of every term and yet there will always be someone who will just go ahead and cheat anyway maybe they don t believe me maybe they weren t listening maybe there are still many successful cheaters that i miss so they are playing the odds the most recent incident was on a minor assignment watch a film write a little essay address some main points a student copied a former student s assignment re arranged a few words and turned it in as their own turnitin flagged it i looked it over and figured out what was going on gave the student a zero and then submitted to my ai office for their consideration i don t get why people cheat my classes aren t that hard it s an intro social science course that i have taught multiple times in the last few years the class average at the end of a term is always a low b when people fail it s typically because they just didn t do what was asked of them i am one of those educators who believes in rewarding effort and process above a specific outcome this is a rant i do not have clear questions but was hoping maybe some of the students who lurk this sub would post in response or perhaps other faculty have thoughts thanks for reading', 'i was getting a lot of this sort of thing not from my lectures but other repeating ai ing grabbing off the web across all of my classes last week i took minutes in each class and talked about academic integrity and academic dishonesty i started off by saying that in all my years of teaching i know the reason students turn to cheating is stress but that doesn t make it ok i then talked about coming to college is coming to a place institution community of learning and that comes with an agreement to act with as is said in my school s student conduct policy the highest level of academic integrity i explained this means doing the work oneself no matter what and asking for help when needed i explained that ai reading outside source material to better understand the reading i teach comp out of a basic reader re iterating what i say just repeating the text re iterating what is said in discussion pulling reviews and watching youtubes etc is all academic dishonesty then i said this is what academic dishonesty is in this class which is basically do the reading and answer the questions with full citations i told that academic dishonesty put my ethics on the line because i was expected to pass cheating students and that it took my time away from giving good feedback to the students who were doing the work with academic integrity a really positive thing happened several students communicated with me that they didn t know why they were in this level of english that they didn t know they could drop the class and start fresh another term some are just plain new to college and in too deep some didn t know there were other levels of english to take before some went to their academic advisors immediately i don t know if they don t know but i made it clear that they do now know in my class what is and isn t cheating to your experience op your situation is exactly why i am not letting students record during class time and i did just post about some of these problems in papers i m grading today sigh']

Name: 72_blue_blue books_books_class
Representative_Docs: ['when i was in college and graduate school students were required to take in person midterms and finals in blue books with zero notes allowed even during my early teaching days i zero notes allowed even during my early teaching days i continued the tradition in the past few years possibly only post covid my colleagues began allowing students to bring a cheat sheet of hand written notes one page one side to in person exams i resisted until students expressed outrage i now allow it but in some cases i am not sure it makes a lick of difference especially when it comes to questions that call for analytical and interpretive connections i understand that pedagogical expectations change over the years among students and my colleagues so i am willing to go with the flow but it still feels like a crutch to me how many people here allow students to bring notes to exams has student expectation shifted during your years of teaching and yes i know i am old school having never given up the blue books but hey in the age of ai they have become fashionable again', 'blue books made me a better teacher since we ve been living in a world in which all take home assignments and online assessments of any kind are easier to complete using large language models instead of traditional methods using critical cognitive processes and understanding okay i like a challenge i built backwards from my course s learning objectives and targeted specific assignments i knew would be prone to academic fraud namely essays musical analysis and concert reports i also realized based on recent written assignments even before chatgpt students could benefit from a refresher on fundamentals first month fifteen minutes can save you fifteen percents on written papers and won t run you more than after talking to a retired colleague who had worked in creative writing i created some preliminary writing assignments in order to ensure that students could write theses structure arguments and summarize in ways a human can understand and with penmanship that doesn t require the rosetta stone i m not a writing instructor but given grad school and phd i can at least play one on tv so i went over what i once went over in elementary middle high school and undergrad writing the good news is that these don t take much time fifteen minutes of a seventy five minute lecture isn t bad especially in the middle of two half hour lectures or activities it s a nice structure plus it s good for my back to wander around the hall and give students feedback including i have to be able to read this to grade this and please don t use a death grip on the pen it doesn t owe you money these assignments were hand written on college rule binder paper i supplied with pens i supplied at the beginning of class and collected at the end i doubt it cost more than i also let them know early about the two concert reports and one essay they re expected to write second month twenty minutes of how to turn outlines which were graded for participation and had my feedback into rough drafts it s blue book time now that students were picking topics that interested them for essays they could get started on how to turn an outline into a rough draft i got a little pushback on this one from students who didn t read the syllabus and were now realizing this was a hand writing class but between me making this easy for them and giving them basically no homework outside of practicing concert attendance and a little reading they seem to appreciate that what i gave them was better than the alternative this is also when i started getting the sense that this wasn t just anti fraud it was pro learning penmanship aside the writing didn t just get better but the thinking behind the writing got better also a few students really stepped up in helping their neighbors third month polished papers and marked blue books the second set of blue books are for final drafts and they contain a little surprise in all of the blue books which i pass out at the start of class and collect at the end of class i put a very slight marking in one of five possible locations using invisible ink nobody at my institution knows i do this other than my department chair students are given time at the end of every class explicitly for papers and written assignments and i give them extra time i m there for feedback and one semester i also was provided a ta who also gave feedback outcome i m on my fifth semester of this now and only one student attempted to switch out their blue book the quality of writing has skyrocketed the test score also in class have gone up and the answers are more thoughtful and my student evals haven t suffered one bit the critical thinking synthesis and original thinking in class suggest to me that there s deeper learning happening in my class now than there has been in previous years by integrating progressive assignments procrastination is far less common back']

Name: 75_assessment_assessments_ai_use
Representative_Docs: ['we can t put the ai genie back in the box if you let students as we all do let students do assignments at home some will use ai some will use if appropriately but some will not the students that use it wrongly need to have the appropriate marks awarded i e the real challenge is catching all the ai use i used to think of ai as the new google now i think of it more as the spell check option in microsoft word ai is just there to be used without thought by many it is up to us to think of assessment that do not let students use ai this mostly means in class assessment i know this doesn t work for classes degrees', 'an unfortunate consequence of ai is that professors are increasingly thinking about their courses too much in terms of assessment and wanting to focus more on authentic assessment in some classes the only reason to have assessment is a because we have to and b it is a means to get students to do what we actually want them to do such as reading and attend class now we get professors harping on the idea that if the assignments in a class could be done by ai it must be a bad class or that we need to work harder to find more creative means of assessment when in fact for some classes quite simple assessment which does not distract from actual learning is absolutely appropriate and the problem is with people and companies giving students or selling students tools to cheat so yes we have to adapt esp if our universities won t go after the companies profiting from this but making it sound like we were already wrong is a little offensive', 'students are already using ai whether we like it or not trying to ban it just pushes it underground and makes the whole thing harder to manage a better move is to bring it into the open and teach them how to actually use it well not just how to get answers but how to think critically with the tool the way to do that is by changing how we structure assignments instead of grading the final product alone we look at the process too how did the student use ai what prompts did they try did they revise and push back or just copy the first output lazy use is easy to spot good use takes effort and judgment this approach works best in upper level undergrad classes grad courses capstones anywhere the student already has some basic grip on the material and can tell when the ai is off it fits fields like writing policy design business compsci basically anything where quick iteration and testing ideas matter intro courses are harder students don t always have enough context to evaluate what ai gives them so they can get led astray but even there you can make it work if you structure things right have them critique ai written texts make them explain what s missing or misleading force them to engage not just submit the goal isn t to let ai do the thinking for them it s to teach them how to steer it how to question it how to use it without becoming dependent on it in the real world that s already a basic skill pretending it s cheating doesn t make it go away it just makes our teaching less relevant']
